Height: 7.7m | burial_chamber = Unknown | artifacts = Steatite trough, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments | period = Early [[5th century]] | designation = Izumi City Historic Site (2003) }} '''Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun''' (дҝЎеӨӘиІқеҗ№еұұеҸӨеўі) is a {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі}} located in {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮ}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|еӨ§йҳӘеәң}}, Japan. It has been designated a historic site by Izumi City. == Overview ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Lineage of Izumi Northern Chief Tombs"Lineage of Izumi Northern Chiefs," in {{ill|Izumi City History Volume 6|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮгҒ®жӯҙеҸІ6}}: Archaeology, Ancient, and Medieval, Izumi City Editorial Committee, Izumi City, Gyosei, 2013, pp. 65вҖ“70. ! colspan="2" | Group !! Tomb name !! Shape !! Size !! Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun is located on a low terrace at the northwestern foot of the {{ill|Shinodayama Hills|ja|дҝЎеӨӘеұұдёҳйҷө}} in southern Osaka. The name "Kaibukiyama" derives from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|жұҹжҲёжҷӮд»Ј}} when farmers gathered here during the Chihara Revolt, using conch shells (hЕҚragai) as signals.Historical Site Information Board, Izumi City, 2005. Partial archaeological investigations have been conducted.ЕҢtani, Takeshi. "Kaibukiyama Kofun," in {{ill|Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|ja|ж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨеўіеӨ§иҫһе…ё}}, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. The mound is in the form of a short-tailed {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі}} with the short tail facing northwest. It measures approximately 60 meters in total length, with the rear circular section having a diameter of 50 meters and a height of 7.7 meters. The mound surface reveals {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|и‘әзҹі}} and {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments (both cylindrical and figurative). Surrounding the mound is a moat. Artifacts recovered include haniwa fragments and a steatite trough. The burial chamber's structure remains unknown. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the early [[5th century]], during the middle period of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. Archaeological studies suggest that the northern and southern burial clusters of Izumi alternated as the primary burial sites for regional chiefs."Report on the Excavation of the ЕҢzono Site," Izumi City Education Board, 2014, pp. 1вҖ“4. In 2003, the mound was designated a historic site by Izumi City.{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/65/shiteibunkazai201804.pdf Izumi City Designated Cultural Properties]}} (Izumi City official website). The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the early [[5th century]], during the middle period of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. Archaeological studies suggest that the northern and southern burial clusters of Izumi alternated as the primary burial sites for regional chiefs."Report on the Excavation of the ЕҢzono Site," Izumi City Education Board, 2014, pp. 1вҖ“4. In 2003, the mound was designated a historic site by Izumi City.{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/65/shiteibunkazai201804.pdf Izumi City Designated Cultural Properties]}} (Izumi City official website). Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun is located on a low terrace at the northwestern foot of the {{ill|Shinodayama Hills|ja|дҝЎеӨӘеұұдёҳйҷө}} in southern Osaka. The name "Kaibukiyama" derives from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|жұҹжҲёжҷӮд»Ј}} when farmers gathered here during the Chihara Revolt, using conch shells (hЕҚragai) as signals.Historical Site Information Board, Izumi City, 2005. Partial archaeological investigations have been conducted.ЕҢtani, Takeshi. "Kaibukiyama Kofun," in {{ill|Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|ja|ж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨеўіеӨ§иҫһе…ё}}, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. The mound is in the form of a short-tailed {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі}} with the short tail facing northwest. It measures approximately 60 meters in total length, with the rear circular section having a diameter of 50 meters and a height of 7.7 meters. The mound surface reveals {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|и‘әзҹі}} and {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments (both cylindrical and figurative). Surrounding the mound is a moat. Artifacts recovered include haniwa fragments and a steatite trough. The burial chamber's structure remains unknown. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the early [[5th century]], during the middle period of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. Archaeological studies suggest that the northern and southern burial clusters of Izumi alternated as the primary burial sites for regional chiefs."Report on the Excavation of the ЕҢzono Site," Izumi City Education Board, 2014, pp. 1вҖ“4. In 2003, the mound was designated a historic site by Izumi City.{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/65/shiteibunkazai201804.pdf Izumi City Designated Cultural Properties]}} (Izumi City official website). File:Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun, zenkei.jpg|Full view of the mound

Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun is located on a low terrace at the northwestern foot of the {{ill|Shinodayama Hills|ja|дҝЎеӨӘеұұдёҳйҷө}} in southern Osaka. The name "Kaibukiyama" derives from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|жұҹжҲёжҷӮд»Ј}} when farmers gathered here during the Chihara Revolt, using conch shells (hЕҚragai) as signals.Historical Site Information Board, Izumi City, 2005. Partial archaeological investigations have been conducted.ЕҢtani, Takeshi. "Kaibukiyama Kofun," in {{ill|Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|ja|ж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨеўіеӨ§иҫһе…ё}}, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. The mound is in the form of a short-tailed {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі}} with the short tail facing northwest. It measures approximately 60 meters in total length, with the rear circular section having a diameter of 50 meters and a height of 7.7 meters. The mound surface reveals {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|и‘әзҹі}} and {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments (both cylindrical and figurative). Surrounding the mound is a moat. Artifacts recovered include haniwa fragments and a steatite trough. The burial chamber's structure remains unknown. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the early [[5th century]], during the middle period of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. Archaeological studies suggest that the northern and southern burial clusters of Izumi alternated as the primary burial sites for regional chiefs."Report on the Excavation of the ЕҢzono Site," Izumi City Education Board, 2014, pp. 1вҖ“4. In 2003, the mound was designated a historic site by Izumi City.{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/65/shiteibunkazai201804.pdf Izumi City Designated Cultural Properties]}} (Izumi City official website). Height: 7.7m | burial_chamber = Unknown | artifacts = Steatite trough, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments | period = Early [[5th century]] | designation = Izumi City Historic Site (2003) }} '''Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun''' (дҝЎеӨӘиІқеҗ№еұұеҸӨеўі) is a {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі}} located in {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮ}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|еӨ§йҳӘеәң}}, Japan. It has been designated a historic site by Izumi City. == Overview ==

Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun is located on a low terrace at the northwestern foot of the {{ill|Shinodayama Hills|ja|дҝЎеӨӘеұұдёҳйҷө}} in southern Osaka. The name "Kaibukiyama" derives from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|жұҹжҲёжҷӮд»Ј}} when farmers gathered here during the Chihara Revolt, using conch shells (hЕҚragai) as signals.Historical Site Information Board, Izumi City, 2005. Partial archaeological investigations have been conducted.ЕҢtani, Takeshi. "Kaibukiyama Kofun," in {{ill|Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|ja|ж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨеўіеӨ§иҫһе…ё}}, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. The mound is in the form of a short-tailed {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі}} with the short tail facing northwest. It measures approximately 60 meters in total length, with the rear circular section having a diameter of 50 meters and a height of 7.7 meters. The mound surface reveals {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|и‘әзҹі}} and {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments (both cylindrical and figurative). Surrounding the mound is a moat. Artifacts recovered include haniwa fragments and a steatite trough. The burial chamber's structure remains unknown. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the early [[5th century]], during the middle period of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. Archaeological studies suggest that the northern and southern burial clusters of Izumi alternated as the primary burial sites for regional chiefs."Report on the Excavation of the ЕҢzono Site," Izumi City Education Board, 2014, pp. 1вҖ“4. In 2003, the mound was designated a historic site by Izumi City.{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/65/shiteibunkazai201804.pdf Izumi City Designated Cultural Properties]}} (Izumi City official website). The name "Kaibukiyama" derives from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|жұҹжҲёжҷӮд»Ј}} when farmers gathered here during the Chihara Revolt, using conch shells (hЕҚragai) as signals.Historical Site Information Board, Izumi City, 2005. Partial archaeological investigations have been conducted.ЕҢtani, Takeshi. "Kaibukiyama Kofun," in {{ill|Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|ja|ж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨеўіеӨ§иҫһе…ё}}, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. The mound is in the form of a short-tailed {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі}} with the short tail facing northwest. It measures approximately 60 meters in total length, with the rear circular section having a diameter of 50 meters and a height of 7.7 meters. The mound surface reveals {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|и‘әзҹі}} and {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments (both cylindrical and figurative). Surrounding the mound is a moat. Artifacts recovered include haniwa fragments and a steatite trough. The burial chamber's structure remains unknown. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the early [[5th century]], during the middle period of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. Archaeological studies suggest that the northern and southern burial clusters of Izumi alternated as the primary burial sites for regional chiefs."Report on the Excavation of the ЕҢzono Site," Izumi City Education Board, 2014, pp. 1вҖ“4. In 2003, the mound was designated a historic site by Izumi City.{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/65/shiteibunkazai201804.pdf Izumi City Designated Cultural Properties]}} (Izumi City official website). Entrance to the mound and informational sign | alternate_name = | group = | location = {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮ}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|еӨ§йҳӘеәң}}, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|30|30.40|N|135|26|32.25|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | type = {{ill|Hotategaigata Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі}} | size = Total mound length: 60m
Height: 7.7m | burial_chamber = Unknown | artifacts = Steatite trough, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments | period = Early [[5th century]] | designation = Izumi City Historic Site (2003) }} '''Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun''' (дҝЎеӨӘиІқеҗ№еұұеҸӨеўі) is a {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі}} located in {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮ}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|еӨ§йҳӘеәң}}, Japan. It has been designated a historic site by Izumi City. == Overview ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Lineage of Izumi Northern Chief Tombs"Lineage of Izumi Northern Chiefs," in {{ill|Izumi City History Volume 6|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮгҒ®жӯҙеҸІ6}}: Archaeology, Ancient, and Medieval, Izumi City Editorial Committee, Izumi City, Gyosei, 2013, pp. 65вҖ“70. ! colspan="2" | Group !! Tomb name !! Shape !! Size !! Period |- | North || || {{ill|Izumi Koganezuka Kofun|ja|е’Ңжіүй»„йҮ‘еЎҡеҸӨеўі}}|| {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}} || 94m || Late 4th century |- | || South || Marukasa-yama Kofun || Zenpokoenfun || 96m || Late 4th century |- | || South || Nabetsuka Kofun || {{ill|Empun|ja|еҶҶеўі}} || 60вҖ“70m || Early 5th century |- | || South || ЕҢtsuka Kofun || Hotategaigata Kofun || 46m || Mid-5th century |- | North || || '''Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun''' || Hotategaigata Kofun || 60m || Early 5th century |- | North || || Kaniya-zuka Kofun || Hotategaigata Kofun || Unknown || Late 5th century |- | North || || {{ill|ЕҢzono Kofun|ja|еӨ§ең’еҸӨеўі}} || Hotategaigata Kofun || 50m || Late 5th century |- | North || || {{ill|Togidensha Kofun|ja|еҜҢжңЁи»ҠеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} || Zenpokoenfun || 48m || Mid-6th century |- | || South || Kitsunezuka Kofun || Zenpokoenfun || 58m || Late 6th century |}
Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun is located on a low terrace at the northwestern foot of the {{ill|Shinodayama Hills|ja|дҝЎеӨӘеұұдёҳйҷө}} in southern Osaka. The name "Kaibukiyama" derives from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|жұҹжҲёжҷӮд»Ј}} when farmers gathered here during the Chihara Revolt, using conch shells (hЕҚragai) as signals.Historical Site Information Board, Izumi City, 2005. Partial archaeological investigations have been conducted.ЕҢtani, Takeshi. "Kaibukiyama Kofun," in {{ill|Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|ja|ж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨеўіеӨ§иҫһе…ё}}, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. The mound is in the form of a short-tailed {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі}} with the short tail facing northwest. It measures approximately 60 meters in total length, with the rear circular section having a diameter of 50 meters and a height of 7.7 meters. The mound surface reveals {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|и‘әзҹі}} and {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments (both cylindrical and figurative). Surrounding the mound is a moat. Artifacts recovered include haniwa fragments and a steatite trough. The burial chamber's structure remains unknown. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the early [[5th century]], during the middle period of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. Archaeological studies suggest that the northern and southern burial clusters of Izumi alternated as the primary burial sites for regional chiefs."Report on the Excavation of the ЕҢzono Site," Izumi City Education Board, 2014, pp. 1вҖ“4. In 2003, the mound was designated a historic site by Izumi City.{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/65/shiteibunkazai201804.pdf Izumi City Designated Cultural Properties]}} (Izumi City official website). Height: 7.7m | burial_chamber = Unknown | artifacts = Steatite trough, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments | period = Early [[5th century]] | designation = Izumi City Historic Site (2003) }} '''Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun''' (дҝЎеӨӘиІқеҗ№еұұеҸӨеўі) is a {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі}} located in {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮ}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|еӨ§йҳӘеәң}}, Japan. It has been designated a historic site by Izumi City. == Overview ==

Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun is located on a low terrace at the northwestern foot of the {{ill|Shinodayama Hills|ja|дҝЎеӨӘеұұдёҳйҷө}} in southern Osaka. The name "Kaibukiyama" derives from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|жұҹжҲёжҷӮд»Ј}} when farmers gathered here during the Chihara Revolt, using conch shells (hЕҚragai) as signals.Historical Site Information Board, Izumi City, 2005. Partial archaeological investigations have been conducted.ЕҢtani, Takeshi. "Kaibukiyama Kofun," in {{ill|Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|ja|ж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨеўіеӨ§иҫһе…ё}}, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. The mound is in the form of a short-tailed {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі}} with the short tail facing northwest. It measures approximately 60 meters in total length, with the rear circular section having a diameter of 50 meters and a height of 7.7 meters. The mound surface reveals {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|и‘әзҹі}} and {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments (both cylindrical and figurative). Surrounding the mound is a moat. Artifacts recovered include haniwa fragments and a steatite trough. The burial chamber's structure remains unknown. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the early [[5th century]], during the middle period of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. Archaeological studies suggest that the northern and southern burial clusters of Izumi alternated as the primary burial sites for regional chiefs."Report on the Excavation of the ЕҢzono Site," Izumi City Education Board, 2014, pp. 1вҖ“4. In 2003, the mound was designated a historic site by Izumi City.{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/65/shiteibunkazai201804.pdf Izumi City Designated Cultural Properties]}} (Izumi City official website). Partial archaeological investigations have been conducted.ЕҢtani, Takeshi. "Kaibukiyama Kofun," in {{ill|Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|ja|ж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨеўіеӨ§иҫһе…ё}}, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. The mound is in the form of a short-tailed {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі}} with the short tail facing northwest. Artifacts recovered include haniwa fragments and a steatite trough. The burial chamber's structure remains unknown. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the early [[5th century]], during the middle period of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. Archaeological studies suggest that the northern and southern burial clusters of Izumi alternated as the primary burial sites for regional chiefs."Report on the Excavation of the ЕҢzono Site," Izumi City Education Board, 2014, pp. 1вҖ“4. The name "Kaibukiyama" derives from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|жұҹжҲёжҷӮд»Ј}} when farmers gathered here during the Chihara Revolt, using conch shells (hЕҚragai) as signals.Historical Site Information Board, Izumi City, 2005. Partial archaeological investigations have been conducted.ЕҢtani, Takeshi. "Kaibukiyama Kofun," in {{ill|Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|ja|ж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨеўіеӨ§иҫһе…ё}}, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. The mound is in the form of a short-tailed {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі}} with the short tail facing northwest. It measures approximately 60 meters in total length, with the rear circular section having a diameter of 50 meters and a height of 7.7 meters. The mound surface reveals {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|и‘әзҹі}} and {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments (both cylindrical and figurative). Surrounding the mound is a moat. Artifacts recovered include haniwa fragments and a steatite trough. The burial chamber's structure remains unknown. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the early [[5th century]], during the middle period of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. Archaeological studies suggest that the northern and southern burial clusters of Izumi alternated as the primary burial sites for regional chiefs."Report on the Excavation of the ЕҢzono Site," Izumi City Education Board, 2014, pp. 1вҖ“4. In 2003, the mound was designated a historic site by Izumi City.{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/65/shiteibunkazai201804.pdf Izumi City Designated Cultural Properties]}} (Izumi City official website). Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun is located on a low terrace at the northwestern foot of the {{ill|Shinodayama Hills|ja|дҝЎеӨӘеұұдёҳйҷө}} in southern Osaka. The name "Kaibukiyama" derives from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|жұҹжҲёжҷӮд»Ј}} when farmers gathered here during the Chihara Revolt, using conch shells (hЕҚragai) as signals.Historical Site Information Board, Izumi City, 2005. Partial archaeological investigations have been conducted.ЕҢtani, Takeshi. "Kaibukiyama Kofun," in {{ill|Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|ja|ж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨеўіеӨ§иҫһе…ё}}, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. The mound is in the form of a short-tailed {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі}} with the short tail facing northwest. It measures approximately 60 meters in total length, with the rear circular section having a diameter of 50 meters and a height of 7.7 meters. The mound surface reveals {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|и‘әзҹі}} and {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments (both cylindrical and figurative). Surrounding the mound is a moat. Artifacts recovered include haniwa fragments and a steatite trough. The burial chamber's structure remains unknown. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the early [[5th century]], during the middle period of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. Archaeological studies suggest that the northern and southern burial clusters of Izumi alternated as the primary burial sites for regional chiefs."Report on the Excavation of the ЕҢzono Site," Izumi City Education Board, 2014, pp. 1вҖ“4. In 2003, the mound was designated a historic site by Izumi City.{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/65/shiteibunkazai201804.pdf Izumi City Designated Cultural Properties]}} (Izumi City official website). {{Infobox ancient site | name = Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun (дҝЎеӨӘиІқеҗ№еұұеҸӨеўі) | image = [[File:Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun, entrance.jpg|280px]]
Entrance to the mound and informational sign | alternate_name = | group = | location = {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮ}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|еӨ§йҳӘеәң}}, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|30|30.40|N|135|26|32.25|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | type = {{ill|Hotategaigata Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі}} | size = Total mound length: 60m
Height: 7.7m | burial_chamber = Unknown | artifacts = Steatite trough, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments | period = Early [[5th century]] | designation = Izumi City Historic Site (2003) }} '''Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun''' (дҝЎеӨӘиІқеҗ№еұұеҸӨеўі) is a {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі}} located in {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮ}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|еӨ§йҳӘеәң}}, Japan. It has been designated a historic site by Izumi City. == Overview ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Lineage of Izumi Northern Chief Tombs"Lineage of Izumi Northern Chiefs," in {{ill|Izumi City History Volume 6|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮгҒ®жӯҙеҸІ6}}: Archaeology, Ancient, and Medieval, Izumi City Editorial Committee, Izumi City, Gyosei, 2013, pp. 65вҖ“70. ! colspan="2" | Group !! Tomb name !! Shape !! Size !! Period |- | North || || {{ill|Izumi Koganezuka Kofun|ja|е’Ңжіүй»„йҮ‘еЎҡеҸӨеўі}}|| {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}} || 94m || Late 4th century |- | || South || Marukasa-yama Kofun || Zenpokoenfun || 96m || Late 4th century |- | || South || Nabetsuka Kofun || {{ill|Empun|ja|еҶҶеўі}} || 60вҖ“70m || Early 5th century |- | || South || ЕҢtsuka Kofun || Hotategaigata Kofun || 46m || Mid-5th century |- | North || || '''Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun''' || Hotategaigata Kofun || 60m || Early 5th century |- | North || || Kaniya-zuka Kofun || Hotategaigata Kofun || Unknown || Late 5th century |- | North || || {{ill|ЕҢzono Kofun|ja|еӨ§ең’еҸӨеўі}} || Hotategaigata Kofun || 50m || Late 5th century |- | North || || {{ill|Togidensha Kofun|ja|еҜҢжңЁи»ҠеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} || Zenpokoenfun || 48m || Mid-6th century |- | || South || Kitsunezuka Kofun || Zenpokoenfun || 58m || Late 6th century |}
Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun is located on a low terrace at the northwestern foot of the {{ill|Shinodayama Hills|ja|дҝЎеӨӘеұұдёҳйҷө}} in southern Osaka. The name "Kaibukiyama" derives from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|жұҹжҲёжҷӮд»Ј}} when farmers gathered here during the Chihara Revolt, using conch shells (hЕҚragai) as signals.Historical Site Information Board, Izumi City, 2005. Partial archaeological investigations have been conducted.ЕҢtani, Takeshi. "Kaibukiyama Kofun," in {{ill|Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|ja|ж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨеўіеӨ§иҫһе…ё}}, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. The mound is in the form of a short-tailed {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі}} with the short tail facing northwest. It measures approximately 60 meters in total length, with the rear circular section having a diameter of 50 meters and a height of 7.7 meters. The mound surface reveals {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|и‘әзҹі}} and {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments (both cylindrical and figurative). Surrounding the mound is a moat. Artifacts recovered include haniwa fragments and a steatite trough. The burial chamber's structure remains unknown. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the early [[5th century]], during the middle period of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. Archaeological studies suggest that the northern and southern burial clusters of Izumi alternated as the primary burial sites for regional chiefs."Report on the Excavation of the ЕҢzono Site," Izumi City Education Board, 2014, pp. 1вҖ“4. In 2003, the mound was designated a historic site by Izumi City.{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/65/shiteibunkazai201804.pdf Izumi City Designated Cultural Properties]}} (Izumi City official website). Entrance to the mound and informational sign | alternate_name = | group = | location = {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮ}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|еӨ§йҳӘеәң}}, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|30|30.40|N|135|26|32.25|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | type = {{ill|Hotategaigata Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі}} | size = Total mound length: 60m
Height: 7.7m | burial_chamber = Unknown | artifacts = Steatite trough, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments | period = Early [[5th century]] | designation = Izumi City Historic Site (2003) }} '''Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun''' (дҝЎеӨӘиІқеҗ№еұұеҸӨеўі) is a {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі}} located in {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮ}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|еӨ§йҳӘеәң}}, Japan. It has been designated a historic site by Izumi City. == Overview ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Lineage of Izumi Northern Chief Tombs"Lineage of Izumi Northern Chiefs," in {{ill|Izumi City History Volume 6|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮгҒ®жӯҙеҸІ6}}: Archaeology, Ancient, and Medieval, Izumi City Editorial Committee, Izumi City, Gyosei, 2013, pp. 65вҖ“70. ! colspan="2" | Group !! Tomb name !! Shape !! Size !! Period |- | North || || {{ill|Izumi Koganezuka Kofun|ja|е’Ңжіүй»„йҮ‘еЎҡеҸӨеўі}}|| {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}} || 94m || Late 4th century |- | || South || Marukasa-yama Kofun || Zenpokoenfun || 96m || Late 4th century |- | || South || Nabetsuka Kofun || {{ill|Empun|ja|еҶҶеўі}} || 60вҖ“70m || Early 5th century |- | || South || ЕҢtsuka Kofun || Hotategaigata Kofun || 46m || Mid-5th century |- | North || || '''Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun''' || Hotategaigata Kofun || 60m || Early 5th century |- | North || || Kaniya-zuka Kofun || Hotategaigata Kofun || Unknown || Late 5th century |- | North || || {{ill|ЕҢzono Kofun|ja|еӨ§ең’еҸӨеўі}} || Hotategaigata Kofun || 50m || Late 5th century |- | North || || {{ill|Togidensha Kofun|ja|еҜҢжңЁи»ҠеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} || Zenpokoenfun || 48m || Mid-6th century |- | || South || Kitsunezuka Kofun || Zenpokoenfun || 58m || Late 6th century |}
Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun is located on a low terrace at the northwestern foot of the {{ill|Shinodayama Hills|ja|дҝЎеӨӘеұұдёҳйҷө}} in southern Osaka. The name "Kaibukiyama" derives from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|жұҹжҲёжҷӮд»Ј}} when farmers gathered here during the Chihara Revolt, using conch shells (hЕҚragai) as signals.Historical Site Information Board, Izumi City, 2005. Partial archaeological investigations have been conducted.ЕҢtani, Takeshi. "Kaibukiyama Kofun," in {{ill|Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|ja|ж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨеўіеӨ§иҫһе…ё}}, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. The mound is in the form of a short-tailed {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі}} with the short tail facing northwest. It measures approximately 60 meters in total length, with the rear circular section having a diameter of 50 meters and a height of 7.7 meters. The mound surface reveals {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|и‘әзҹі}} and {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments (both cylindrical and figurative). Surrounding the mound is a moat. Artifacts recovered include haniwa fragments and a steatite trough. The burial chamber's structure remains unknown. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the early [[5th century]], during the middle period of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. Archaeological studies suggest that the northern and southern burial clusters of Izumi alternated as the primary burial sites for regional chiefs."Report on the Excavation of the ЕҢzono Site," Izumi City Education Board, 2014, pp. 1вҖ“4. In 2003, the mound was designated a historic site by Izumi City.{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/65/shiteibunkazai201804.pdf Izumi City Designated Cultural Properties]}} (Izumi City official website). Entrance to the mound and informational sign | alternate_name = | group = | location = {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮ}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|еӨ§йҳӘеәң}}, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|30|30.40|N|135|26|32.25|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | type = {{ill|Hotategaigata Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} | size = Total mound length: 60m
Height: 7.7m | burial_chamber = Unknown | artifacts = Steatite trough, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments | period = Early [[5th century]] | designation = Izumi City Historic Site (2003) }} '''Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun''' (дҝЎеӨӘиІқеҗ№еұұеҸӨеўі) is a {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} located in {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮ}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|еӨ§йҳӘеәң}}, Japan. It has been designated a historic site by Izumi City. == Overview ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Lineage of Izumi Northern Chief Tombs"Lineage of Izumi Northern Chiefs," in {{ill|Izumi City History Volume 6|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮгҒ®жӯҙеҸІ6}}: Archaeology, Ancient, and Medieval, Izumi City Editorial Committee, Izumi City, Gyosei, 2013, pp. 65вҖ“70. ! colspan="2" | Group !! Tomb name !! Shape !! Size !! Period |- | North || || {{ill|Izumi Koganezuka Kofun|ja|е’Ңжіүй»„йҮ‘еЎҡеҸӨеўі}}|| {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}} || 94m || Late 4th century |- | || South || Marukasa-yama Kofun || Zenpokoenfun || 96m || Late 4th century |- | || South || Nabetsuka Kofun || {{ill|Empun|ja|еҶҶеўі|1=Round Kofun}} || 60вҖ“70m || Early 5th century |- | || South || ЕҢtsuka Kofun || Hotategaigata Kofun || 46m || Mid-5th century |- | North || || '''Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun''' || Hotategaigata Kofun || 60m || Early 5th century |- | North || || Kaniya-zuka Kofun || Hotategaigata Kofun || Unknown || Late 5th century |- | North || || {{ill|ЕҢzono Kofun|ja|еӨ§ең’еҸӨеўі}} || Hotategaigata Kofun || 50m || Late 5th century |- | North || || {{ill|Togidensha Kofun|ja|еҜҢжңЁи»ҠеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} || Zenpokoenfun || 48m || Mid-6th century |- | || South || Kitsunezuka Kofun || Zenpokoenfun || 58m || Late 6th century |}
Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun is located on a low terrace at the northwestern foot of the {{ill|Shinodayama Hills|ja|дҝЎеӨӘеұұдёҳйҷө}} in southern Osaka. The name "Kaibukiyama" derives from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|жұҹжҲёжҷӮд»Ј}} when farmers gathered here during the Chihara Revolt, using conch shells (hЕҚragai) as signals.Historical Site Information Board, Izumi City, 2005. Partial archaeological investigations have been conducted.ЕҢtani, Takeshi. "Kaibukiyama Kofun," in {{ill|Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|ja|ж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨеўіеӨ§иҫһе…ё}}, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. The mound is in the form of a short-tailed {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} with the short tail facing northwest. It measures approximately 60 meters in total length, with the rear circular section having a diameter of 50 meters and a height of 7.7 meters. The mound surface reveals {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|и‘әзҹі}} and {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments (both cylindrical and figurative). Surrounding the mound is a moat. Artifacts recovered include haniwa fragments and a steatite trough. The burial chamber's structure remains unknown. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the early [[5th century]], during the middle period of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. Archaeological studies suggest that the northern and southern burial clusters of Izumi alternated as the primary burial sites for regional chiefs."Report on the Excavation of the ЕҢzono Site," Izumi City Education Board, 2014, pp. 1вҖ“4. In 2003, the mound was designated a historic site by Izumi City.{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/65/shiteibunkazai201804.pdf Izumi City Designated Cultural Properties]}} (Izumi City official website). Entrance to the mound and informational sign | alternate_name = | group = | location = {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮ}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|еӨ§йҳӘеәң}}, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|30|30.40|N|135|26|32.25|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | type = {{ill|Hotategaigata Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} | size = Total mound length: 60m
Height: 7.7m | burial_chamber = Unknown | artifacts = Steatite trough, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments | period = Early [[5th century]] | designation = Izumi City Historic Site (2003) }} '''Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun''' (дҝЎеӨӘиІқеҗ№еұұеҸӨеўі) is a {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} located in {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮ}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|еӨ§йҳӘеәң}}, Japan. It has been designated a historic site by Izumi City. == Overview ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Lineage of Izumi Northern Chief Tombs"Lineage of Izumi Northern Chiefs," in {{ill|Izumi City History Volume 6|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮгҒ®жӯҙеҸІ6}}: Archaeology, Ancient, and Medieval, Izumi City Editorial Committee, Izumi City, Gyosei, 2013, pp. 65вҖ“70. ! colspan="2" | Group !! Tomb name !! Shape !! Size !! Period |- | North || || {{ill|Izumi Koganezuka Kofun|ja|е’Ңжіүй»„йҮ‘еЎҡеҸӨеўі}}|| {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}} || 94m || Late 4th century |- | || South || Marukasa-yama Kofun || Zenpokoenfun || 96m || Late 4th century |- | || South || Nabetsuka Kofun || {{ill|Empun|ja|еҶҶеўі|1=Round Kofun}} || 60вҖ“70m || Early 5th century |- | || South || ЕҢtsuka Kofun || Hotategaigata Kofun || 46m || Mid-5th century |- | North || || '''Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun''' || Hotategaigata Kofun || 60m || Early 5th century |- | North || || Kaniya-zuka Kofun || Hotategaigata Kofun || Unknown || Late 5th century |- | North || || {{ill|ЕҢzono Kofun|ja|еӨ§ең’еҸӨеўі}} || Hotategaigata Kofun || 50m || Late 5th century |- | North || || {{ill|Togidensha Kofun|ja|еҜҢжңЁи»ҠеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} || Zenpokoenfun || 48m || Mid-6th century |- | || South || Kitsunezuka Kofun || Zenpokoenfun || 58m || Late 6th century |}
Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun is located on a low terrace at the northwestern foot of the {{ill|Shinodayama Hills|ja|дҝЎеӨӘеұұдёҳйҷө}} in southern Osaka. The name "Kaibukiyama" derives from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|жұҹжҲёжҷӮд»Ј}} when farmers gathered here during the Chihara Revolt, using conch shells (hЕҚragai) as signals.Historical Site Information Board, Izumi City, 2005. Partial archaeological investigations have been conducted.ЕҢtani, Takeshi. "Kaibukiyama Kofun," in {{ill|Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|ja|ж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨеўіеӨ§иҫһе…ё}}, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. The mound is in the form of a short-tailed {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} with the short tail facing northwest. It measures approximately 60 meters in total length, with the rear circular section having a diameter of 50 meters and a height of 7.7 meters. The mound surface reveals {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|и‘әзҹі}} and {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments (both cylindrical and figurative). Surrounding the mound is a moat. Artifacts recovered include haniwa fragments and a steatite trough. The burial chamber's structure remains unknown. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the early [[5th century]], during the middle period of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. Archaeological studies suggest that the northern and southern burial clusters of Izumi alternated as the primary burial sites for regional chiefs."Report on the Excavation of the ЕҢzono Site," Izumi City Education Board, 2014, pp. 1вҖ“4. In 2003, the mound was designated a historic site by Izumi City.{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/65/shiteibunkazai201804.pdf Izumi City Designated Cultural Properties]}} (Izumi City official website). Height: 7.7m | burial_chamber = Unknown | artifacts = Steatite trough, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments | period = Early [[5th century]] | designation = Izumi City Historic Site (2003) }} '''Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun''' (дҝЎеӨӘиІқеҗ№еұұеҸӨеўі) is a {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} located in {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮ}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|еӨ§йҳӘеәң}}, Japan. It has been designated a historic site by Izumi City. == Overview ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Lineage of Izumi Northern Chief Tombs"Lineage of Izumi Northern Chiefs," in {{ill|Izumi City History Volume 6|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮгҒ®жӯҙеҸІ6}}: Archaeology, Ancient, and Medieval, Izumi City Editorial Committee, Izumi City, Gyosei, 2013, pp. 65вҖ“70. ! colspan="2" | Group !! Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun is located on a low terrace at the northwestern foot of the {{ill|Shinodayama Hills|ja|дҝЎеӨӘеұұдёҳйҷө}} in southern Osaka. The name "Kaibukiyama" derives from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|жұҹжҲёжҷӮд»Ј}} when farmers gathered here during the Chihara Revolt, using conch shells (hЕҚragai) as signals.Historical Site Information Board, Izumi City, 2005. Partial archaeological investigations have been conducted.ЕҢtani, Takeshi. "Kaibukiyama Kofun," in {{ill|Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|ja|ж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨеўіеӨ§иҫһе…ё}}, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. The mound is in the form of a short-tailed {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} with the short tail facing northwest. It measures approximately 60 meters in total length, with the rear circular section having a diameter of 50 meters and a height of 7.7 meters. The mound surface reveals {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|и‘әзҹі}} and {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments (both cylindrical and figurative). Surrounding the mound is a moat. Artifacts recovered include haniwa fragments and a steatite trough. The burial chamber's structure remains unknown. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the early [[5th century]], during the middle period of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. Archaeological studies suggest that the northern and southern burial clusters of Izumi alternated as the primary burial sites for regional chiefs."Report on the Excavation of the ЕҢzono Site," Izumi City Education Board, 2014, pp. 1вҖ“4. In 2003, the mound was designated a historic site by Izumi City.{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/65/shiteibunkazai201804.pdf Izumi City Designated Cultural Properties]}} (Izumi City official website). Height: 7.7m | burial_chamber = Unknown | artifacts = Steatite trough, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments | period = Early [[5th century]] | designation = Izumi City Historic Site (2003) }} '''Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun''' (дҝЎеӨӘиІқеҗ№еұұеҸӨеўі) is a {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} located in {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮ}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|еӨ§йҳӘеәң}}, Japan. It has been designated a historic site by Izumi City. == Overview ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Lineage of Izumi Northern Chief Tombs"Lineage of Izumi Northern Chiefs," in {{ill|Izumi City History Volume 6|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮгҒ®жӯҙеҸІ6}}: Archaeology, Ancient, and Medieval, Izumi City Editorial Committee, Izumi City, Gyosei, 2013, pp. 65вҖ“70. ! colspan="2" | Group !! Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun is located on a low terrace at the northwestern foot of the {{ill|Shinodayama Hills|ja|дҝЎеӨӘеұұдёҳйҷө}} in southern Osaka. The name "Kaibukiyama" derives from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|жұҹжҲёжҷӮд»Ј}} when farmers gathered here during the Chihara Revolt, using conch shells (hЕҚragai) as signals.Historical Site Information Board, Izumi City, 2005. Partial archaeological investigations have been conducted.ЕҢtani, Takeshi. "Kaibukiyama Kofun," in {{ill|Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|ja|ж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨеўіеӨ§иҫһе…ё}}, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. The mound is in the form of a short-tailed {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} with the short tail facing northwest. It measures approximately 60 meters in total length, with the rear circular section having a diameter of 50 meters and a height of 7.7 meters. The mound surface reveals {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|и‘әзҹі}} and {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments (both cylindrical and figurative). Surrounding the mound is a moat. Artifacts recovered include haniwa fragments and a steatite trough. The burial chamber's structure remains unknown. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the early [[5th century]], during the middle period of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. Archaeological studies suggest that the northern and southern burial clusters of Izumi alternated as the primary burial sites for regional chiefs."Report on the Excavation of the ЕҢzono Site," Izumi City Education Board, 2014, pp. 1вҖ“4. In 2003, the mound was designated a historic site by Izumi City.{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/65/shiteibunkazai201804.pdf Izumi City Designated Cultural Properties]}} (Izumi City official website). Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun is located on a low terrace at the northwestern foot of the {{ill|Shinodayama Hills|ja|дҝЎеӨӘеұұдёҳйҷө}} in southern Osaka. The name "Kaibukiyama" derives from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|жұҹжҲёжҷӮд»Ј}} when farmers gathered here during the Chihara Revolt, using conch shells (hЕҚragai) as signals.Historical Site Information Board, Izumi City, 2005. Partial archaeological investigations have been conducted.ЕҢtani, Takeshi. "Kaibukiyama Kofun," in {{ill|Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|ja|ж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨеўіеӨ§иҫһе…ё}}, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. The mound is in the form of a short-tailed {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} with the short tail facing northwest. It measures approximately 60 meters in total length, with the rear circular section having a diameter of 50 meters and a height of 7.7 meters. The mound surface reveals {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|и‘әзҹі}} and {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments (both cylindrical and figurative). Surrounding the mound is a moat. Artifacts recovered include haniwa fragments and a steatite trough. The burial chamber's structure remains unknown. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the early [[5th century]], during the middle period of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. Archaeological studies suggest that the northern and southern burial clusters of Izumi alternated as the primary burial sites for regional chiefs."Report on the Excavation of the ЕҢzono Site," Izumi City Education Board, 2014, pp. 1вҖ“4. In 2003, the mound was designated a historic site by Izumi City.{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/65/shiteibunkazai201804.pdf Izumi City Designated Cultural Properties]}} (Izumi City official website). Height: 7.7m | burial_chamber = Unknown | artifacts = Steatite trough, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments | period = Early [[5th century]] | designation = Izumi City Historic Site (2003) }} '''Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun''' (дҝЎеӨӘиІқеҗ№еұұеҸӨеўі) is a {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} located in {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮ}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|еӨ§йҳӘеәң}}, Japan. It has been designated a historic site by Izumi City. == Overview ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Lineage of Izumi Northern Chief Tombs"Lineage of Izumi Northern Chiefs," in {{ill|Izumi City History Volume 6|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮгҒ®жӯҙеҸІ6}}: Archaeology, Ancient, and Medieval, Izumi City Editorial Committee, Izumi City, Gyosei, 2013, pp. 65вҖ“70. ! colspan="2" | Group !! Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun is located on a low terrace at the northwestern foot of the {{ill|Shinodayama Hills|ja|дҝЎеӨӘеұұдёҳйҷө}} in southern Osaka. The name "Kaibukiyama" derives from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|жұҹжҲёжҷӮд»Ј}} when farmers gathered here during the Chihara Revolt, using conch shells (hЕҚragai) as signals.Historical Site Information Board, Izumi City, 2005. Partial archaeological investigations have been conducted.ЕҢtani, Takeshi. "Kaibukiyama Kofun," in {{ill|Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|ja|ж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨеўіеӨ§иҫһе…ё}}, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. The mound is in the form of a short-tailed {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} with the short tail facing northwest. It measures approximately 60 meters in total length, with the rear circular section having a diameter of 50 meters and a height of 7.7 meters. The mound surface reveals {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|и‘әзҹі}} and {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments (both cylindrical and figurative). Surrounding the mound is a moat. Artifacts recovered include haniwa fragments and a steatite trough. The burial chamber's structure remains unknown. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the early [[5th century]], during the middle period of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. Archaeological studies suggest that the northern and southern burial clusters of Izumi alternated as the primary burial sites for regional chiefs."Report on the Excavation of the ЕҢzono Site," Izumi City Education Board, 2014, pp. 1вҖ“4. In 2003, the mound was designated a historic site by Izumi City.{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/65/shiteibunkazai201804.pdf Izumi City Designated Cultural Properties]}} (Izumi City official website). Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun is located on a low terrace at the northwestern foot of the {{ill|Shinodayama Hills|ja|дҝЎеӨӘеұұдёҳйҷө}} in southern Osaka. The name "Kaibukiyama" derives from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|жұҹжҲёжҷӮд»Ј}} when farmers gathered here during the Chihara Revolt, using conch shells (hЕҚragai) as signals.Historical Site Information Board, Izumi City, 2005. "Kaibukiyama Kofun," in {{ill|Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|ja|ж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨеўіеӨ§иҫһе…ё}}, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. The mound is in the form of a short-tailed {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} with the short tail facing northwest. The mound surface reveals {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|и‘әзҹі}} and {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments (both cylindrical and figurative). Surrounding the mound is a moat. Artifacts recovered include haniwa fragments and a steatite trough. The burial chamber's structure remains unknown. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the early [[5th century]], during the middle period of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. Archaeological studies suggest that the northern and southern burial clusters of Izumi alternated as the primary burial sites for regional chiefs."Report on the Excavation of the ЕҢzono Site," Izumi City Education Board, 2014, pp. 1вҖ“4. In 2003, the mound was designated a historic site by Izumi City.{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/65/shiteibunkazai201804.pdf Izumi City Designated Cultural Properties]}} (Izumi City official website). Height: 7.7m | burial_chamber = Unknown | artifacts = Steatite trough, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments | period = Early [[5th century]] | designation = Izumi City Historic Site (2003) }} '''Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun''' (дҝЎеӨӘиІқеҗ№еұұеҸӨеўі) is a {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} located in {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮ}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|еӨ§йҳӘеәң}}, Japan. It has been designated a historic site by Izumi City. == Overview ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Lineage of Izumi Northern Chief Tombs"Lineage of Izumi Northern Chiefs," in {{ill|Izumi City History Volume 6|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮгҒ®жӯҙеҸІ6}}: Archaeology, Ancient, and Medieval, Izumi City Editorial Committee, Izumi City, Gyosei, 2013, pp. 65вҖ“70. ! colspan="2" | Group !! Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun is located on a low terrace at the northwestern foot of the {{ill|Shinodayama Hills|ja|дҝЎеӨӘеұұдёҳйҷө}} in southern Osaka. The name "Kaibukiyama" derives from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|жұҹжҲёжҷӮд»Ј}} when farmers gathered here during the Chihara Revolt, using conch shells (hЕҚragai) as signals.Historical Site Information Board, Izumi City, 2005. Partial archaeological investigations have been conducted.ЕҢtani, Takeshi. "Kaibukiyama Kofun," in {{ill|Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|ja|ж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨеўіеӨ§иҫһе…ё}}, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. The mound is in the form of a short-tailed {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} with the short tail facing northwest. It measures approximately 60 meters in total length, with the rear circular section having a diameter of 50 meters and a height of 7.7 meters. The mound surface reveals {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|и‘әзҹі}} and {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments (both cylindrical and figurative). Surrounding the mound is a moat. Artifacts recovered include haniwa fragments and a steatite trough. The burial chamber's structure remains unknown. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the early [[5th century]], during the middle period of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. Archaeological studies suggest that the northern and southern burial clusters of Izumi alternated as the primary burial sites for regional chiefs."Report on the Excavation of the ЕҢzono Site," Izumi City Education Board, 2014, pp. 1вҖ“4. In 2003, the mound was designated a historic site by Izumi City.{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/65/shiteibunkazai201804.pdf Izumi City Designated Cultural Properties]}} (Izumi City official website). Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun is located on a low terrace at the northwestern foot of the {{ill|Shinodayama Hills|ja|дҝЎеӨӘеұұдёҳйҷө}} in southern Osaka. The name "Kaibukiyama" derives from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|жұҹжҲёжҷӮд»Ј}} when farmers gathered here during the Chihara Revolt, using conch shells (hЕҚragai) as signals.Historical Site Information Board, Izumi City, 2005. Partial archaeological investigations have been conducted.ЕҢtani, Takeshi. "Kaibukiyama Kofun," in {{ill|Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|ja|ж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨеўіеӨ§иҫһе…ё}}, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. The mound is in the form of a short-tailed {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} with the short tail facing northwest. It measures approximately 60 meters in total length, with the rear circular section having a diameter of 50 meters and a height of 7.7 meters. The mound surface reveals {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|и‘әзҹі}} and {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments (both cylindrical and figurative). Surrounding the mound is a moat. Artifacts recovered include haniwa fragments and a steatite trough. The burial chamber's structure remains unknown. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the early [[5th century]], during the middle period of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. Archaeological studies suggest that the northern and southern burial clusters of Izumi alternated as the primary burial sites for regional chiefs."Report on the Excavation of the ЕҢzono Site," Izumi City Education Board, 2014, pp. 1вҖ“4. In 2003, the mound was designated a historic site by Izumi City.{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/65/shiteibunkazai201804.pdf Izumi City Designated Cultural Properties]}} (Izumi City official website). Entrance to the mound and informational sign | alternate_name = | group = | location = {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮ}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|еӨ§йҳӘеәң}}, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|30|30.40|N|135|26|32.25|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | type = {{ill|Hotategaigata Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} | size = Total mound length: 60m
Height: 7.7m | burial_chamber = Unknown | artifacts = Steatite trough, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments | period = Early [[5th century]] | designation = Izumi City Historic Site (2003) }} '''Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun''' (дҝЎеӨӘиІқеҗ№еұұеҸӨеўі) is a {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} located in {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮ}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|еӨ§йҳӘеәң}}, Japan. It has been designated a historic site by Izumi City. == Overview ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Lineage of Izumi Northern Chief Tombs"Lineage of Izumi Northern Chiefs," in {{ill|Izumi City History Volume 6|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮгҒ®жӯҙеҸІ6}}: Archaeology, Ancient, and Medieval, Izumi City Editorial Committee, Izumi City, Gyosei, 2013, pp. 65вҖ“70. ! colspan="2" | Group !! Tomb name !! Shape !! Size !! Period |- | North || || {{ill|Izumi Koganezuka Kofun|ja|е’Ңжіүй»„йҮ‘еЎҡеҸӨеўі}}|| {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}} || 94m || Late 4th century |- | || South || Marukasa-yama Kofun || Zenpokoenfun || 96m || Late 4th century |- | || South || Nabetsuka Kofun || {{ill|Empun|ja|еҶҶеўі|1=Round Kofun}} || 60вҖ“70m || Early 5th century |- | || South || ЕҢtsuka Kofun || Hotategaigata Kofun || 46m || Mid-5th century |- | North || || '''Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun''' || Hotategaigata Kofun || 60m || Early 5th century |- | North || || Kaniya-zuka Kofun || Hotategaigata Kofun || Unknown || Late 5th century |- | North || || {{ill|ЕҢzono Kofun|ja|еӨ§ең’еҸӨеўі}} || Hotategaigata Kofun || 50m || Late 5th century |- | North || || {{ill|Togidensha Kofun|ja|еҜҢжңЁи»ҠеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} || Zenpokoenfun || 48m || Mid-6th century |- | || South || Kitsunezuka Kofun || Zenpokoenfun || 58m || Late 6th century |}
Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun is located on a low terrace at the northwestern foot of the {{ill|Shinodayama Hills|ja|дҝЎеӨӘеұұдёҳйҷө}} in southern Osaka. The name "Kaibukiyama" derives from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|жұҹжҲёжҷӮд»Ј}} when farmers gathered here during the Chihara Revolt, using conch shells (hЕҚragai) as signals.Historical Site Information Board, Izumi City, 2005. Partial archaeological investigations have been conducted.ЕҢtani, Takeshi. "Kaibukiyama Kofun," in {{ill|Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|ja|ж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨеўіеӨ§иҫһе…ё}}, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. The mound is in the form of a short-tailed {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} with the short tail facing northwest. It measures approximately 60 meters in total length, with the rear circular section having a diameter of 50 meters and a height of 7.7 meters. The mound surface reveals {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|и‘әзҹі}} and {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments (both cylindrical and figurative). Surrounding the mound is a moat. Artifacts recovered include haniwa fragments and a steatite trough. The burial chamber's structure remains unknown. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the early [[5th century]], during the middle period of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. Archaeological studies suggest that the northern and southern burial clusters of Izumi alternated as the primary burial sites for regional chiefs."Report on the Excavation of the ЕҢzono Site," Izumi City Education Board, 2014, pp. 1вҖ“4. In 2003, the mound was designated a historic site by Izumi City.{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/65/shiteibunkazai201804.pdf Izumi City Designated Cultural Properties]}} (Izumi City official website). Height: 7.7m | burial_chamber = Unknown | artifacts = Steatite trough, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments | period = Early [[5th century]] | designation = Izumi City Historic Site (2003) }} '''Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun''' (дҝЎеӨӘиІқеҗ№еұұеҸӨеўі) is a {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} located in {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮ}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|еӨ§йҳӘеәң}}, Japan. It has been designated a historic site by Izumi City. == Overview ==

Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun is located on a low terrace at the northwestern foot of the {{ill|Shinodayama Hills|ja|дҝЎеӨӘеұұдёҳйҷө}} in southern Osaka. The name "Kaibukiyama" derives from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|жұҹжҲёжҷӮд»Ј}} when farmers gathered here during the Chihara Revolt, using conch shells (hЕҚragai) as signals.Historical Site Information Board, Izumi City, 2005. Partial archaeological investigations have been conducted.ЕҢtani, Takeshi. "Kaibukiyama Kofun," in {{ill|Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|ja|ж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨеўіеӨ§иҫһе…ё}}, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. The mound is in the form of a short-tailed {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} with the short tail facing northwest. It measures approximately 60 meters in total length, with the rear circular section having a diameter of 50 meters and a height of 7.7 meters. The mound surface reveals {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|и‘әзҹі}} and {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments (both cylindrical and figurative). Surrounding the mound is a moat. Artifacts recovered include haniwa fragments and a steatite trough. The burial chamber's structure remains unknown. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the early [[5th century]], during the middle period of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. Archaeological studies suggest that the northern and southern burial clusters of Izumi alternated as the primary burial sites for regional chiefs."Report on the Excavation of the ЕҢzono Site," Izumi City Education Board, 2014, pp. 1вҖ“4. In 2003, the mound was designated a historic site by Izumi City.{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/65/shiteibunkazai201804.pdf Izumi City Designated Cultural Properties]}} (Izumi City official website). Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun is located on a low terrace at the northwestern foot of the {{ill|Shinodayama Hills|ja|дҝЎеӨӘеұұдёҳйҷө}} in southern Osaka. The name "Kaibukiyama" derives from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|жұҹжҲёжҷӮд»Ј}} when farmers gathered here during the Chihara Revolt, using conch shells (hЕҚragai) as signals.Historical Site Information Board, Izumi City, 2005. Partial archaeological investigations have been conducted.ЕҢtani, Takeshi. "Kaibukiyama Kofun," in {{ill|Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|ja|ж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨеўіеӨ§иҫһе…ё}}, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. The mound is in the form of a short-tailed {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} with the short tail facing northwest. It measures approximately 60 meters in total length, with the rear circular section having a diameter of 50 meters and a height of 7.7 meters. The mound surface reveals {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|и‘әзҹі}} and {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments (both cylindrical and figurative). Surrounding the mound is a moat. Artifacts recovered include haniwa fragments and a steatite trough. The burial chamber's structure remains unknown. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the early [[5th century]], during the middle period of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. Archaeological studies suggest that the northern and southern burial clusters of Izumi alternated as the primary burial sites for regional chiefs."Report on the Excavation of the ЕҢzono Site," Izumi City Education Board, 2014, pp. 1вҖ“4. In 2003, the mound was designated a historic site by Izumi City.{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/65/shiteibunkazai201804.pdf Izumi City Designated Cultural Properties]}} (Izumi City official website). Height: 7.7m | burial_chamber = Unknown | artifacts = Steatite trough, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments | period = Early [[5th century]] | designation = Izumi City Historic Site (2003) }} '''Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun''' (дҝЎеӨӘиІқеҗ№еұұеҸӨеўі) is a {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} located in {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮ}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|еӨ§йҳӘеәң}}, Japan. It has been designated a historic site by Izumi City. == Overview ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Lineage of Izumi Northern Chief Tombs"Lineage of Izumi Northern Chiefs," in {{ill|Izumi City History Volume 6|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮгҒ®жӯҙеҸІ6}}: Archaeology, Ancient, and Medieval, Izumi City Editorial Committee, Izumi City, Gyosei, 2013, pp. 65вҖ“70. ! colspan="2" | Group !! Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun is located on a low terrace at the northwestern foot of the {{ill|Shinodayama Hills|ja|дҝЎеӨӘеұұдёҳйҷө}} in southern Osaka. The name "Kaibukiyama" derives from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|жұҹжҲёжҷӮд»Ј}} when farmers gathered here during the Chihara Revolt, using conch shells (hЕҚragai) as signals.Historical Site Information Board, Izumi City, 2005. Partial archaeological investigations have been conducted.ЕҢtani, Takeshi. "Kaibukiyama Kofun," in {{ill|Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|ja|ж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨеўіеӨ§иҫһе…ё}}, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. The mound is in the form of a short-tailed {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} with the short tail facing northwest. It measures approximately 60 meters in total length, with the rear circular section having a diameter of 50 meters and a height of 7.7 meters. The mound surface reveals {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|и‘әзҹі}} and {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments (both cylindrical and figurative). Surrounding the mound is a moat. Artifacts recovered include haniwa fragments and a steatite trough. The burial chamber's structure remains unknown. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the early [[5th century]], during the middle period of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. Archaeological studies suggest that the northern and southern burial clusters of Izumi alternated as the primary burial sites for regional chiefs."Report on the Excavation of the ЕҢzono Site," Izumi City Education Board, 2014, pp. 1вҖ“4. In 2003, the mound was designated a historic site by Izumi City.{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/65/shiteibunkazai201804.pdf Izumi City Designated Cultural Properties]}} (Izumi City official website). Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun is located on a low terrace at the northwestern foot of the {{ill|Shinodayama Hills|ja|дҝЎеӨӘеұұдёҳйҷө}} in southern Osaka. The name "Kaibukiyama" derives from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|жұҹжҲёжҷӮд»Ј}} when farmers gathered here during the Chihara Revolt, using conch shells (hЕҚragai) as signals.Historical Site Information Board, Izumi City, 2005. Partial archaeological investigations have been conducted.ЕҢtani, Takeshi. "Kaibukiyama Kofun," in {{ill|Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|ja|ж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨеўіеӨ§иҫһе…ё}}, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. The mound is in the form of a short-tailed {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} with the short tail facing northwest. It measures approximately 60 meters in total length, with the rear circular section having a diameter of 50 meters and a height of 7.7 meters. The mound surface reveals {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|и‘әзҹі}} and {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments (both cylindrical and figurative). Surrounding the mound is a moat. Artifacts recovered include haniwa fragments and a steatite trough. The burial chamber's structure remains unknown. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the early [[5th century]], during the middle period of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. Archaeological studies suggest that the northern and southern burial clusters of Izumi alternated as the primary burial sites for regional chiefs."Report on the Excavation of the ЕҢzono Site," Izumi City Education Board, 2014, pp. 1вҖ“4. In 2003, the mound was designated a historic site by Izumi City.{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/65/shiteibunkazai201804.pdf Izumi City Designated Cultural Properties]}} (Izumi City official website). {{Infobox ancient site | name = Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun (дҝЎеӨӘиІқеҗ№еұұеҸӨеўі) | image = [[File:Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun, entrance.jpg|280px]]
Entrance to the mound and informational sign | alternate_name = | group = | location = {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮ}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|еӨ§йҳӘеәң}}, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|30|30.40|N|135|26|32.25|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | type = {{ill|Hotategaigata Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} | size = Total mound length: 60m
Height: 7.7m | burial_chamber = Unknown | artifacts = Steatite trough, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments | period = Early [[5th century]] | designation = Izumi City Historic Site (2003) }} '''Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun''' (дҝЎеӨӘиІқеҗ№еұұеҸӨеўі) is a {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} located in {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮ}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|еӨ§йҳӘеәң}}, Japan. It has been designated a historic site by Izumi City. == Overview ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Lineage of Izumi Northern Chief Tombs"Lineage of Izumi Northern Chiefs," in {{ill|Izumi City History Volume 6|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮгҒ®жӯҙеҸІ6}}: Archaeology, Ancient, and Medieval, Izumi City Editorial Committee, Izumi City, Gyosei, 2013, pp. 65вҖ“70. ! colspan="2" | Group !! Tomb name !! Shape !! Size !! Period |- | North || || {{ill|Izumi Koganezuka Kofun|ja|е’Ңжіүй»„йҮ‘еЎҡеҸӨеўі}}|| {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|1=Zenpokoenfun}} || 94m || Late 4th century |- | || South || Marukasa-yama Kofun || Zenpokoenfun || 96m || Late 4th century |- | || South || Nabetsuka Kofun || {{ill|Empun|ja|еҶҶеўі|1=Round Kofun}} || 60вҖ“70m || Early 5th century |- | || South || ЕҢtsuka Kofun || Hotategaigata Kofun || 46m || Mid-5th century |- | North || || '''Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun''' || Hotategaigata Kofun || 60m || Early 5th century |- | North || || Kaniya-zuka Kofun || Hotategaigata Kofun || Unknown || Late 5th century |- | North || || {{ill|ЕҢzono Kofun|ja|еӨ§ең’еҸӨеўі}} || Hotategaigata Kofun || 50m || Late 5th century |- | North || || {{ill|Togidensha Kofun|ja|еҜҢжңЁи»ҠеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} || Zenpokoenfun || 48m || Mid-6th century |- | || South || Kitsunezuka Kofun || Zenpokoenfun || 58m || Late 6th century |}
Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun is located on a low terrace at the northwestern foot of the {{ill|Shinodayama Hills|ja|дҝЎеӨӘеұұдёҳйҷө}} in southern Osaka. The name "Kaibukiyama" derives from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|жұҹжҲёжҷӮд»Ј}} when farmers gathered here during the Chihara Revolt, using conch shells (hЕҚragai) as signals.Historical Site Information Board, Izumi City, 2005. Partial archaeological investigations have been conducted.ЕҢtani, Takeshi. "Kaibukiyama Kofun," in {{ill|Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|ja|ж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨеўіеӨ§иҫһе…ё}}, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. The mound is in the form of a short-tailed {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} with the short tail facing northwest. It measures approximately 60 meters in total length, with the rear circular section having a diameter of 50 meters and a height of 7.7 meters. The mound surface reveals {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|и‘әзҹі}} and {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments (both cylindrical and figurative). Surrounding the mound is a moat. Artifacts recovered include haniwa fragments and a steatite trough. The burial chamber's structure remains unknown. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the early [[5th century]], during the middle period of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. Archaeological studies suggest that the northern and southern burial clusters of Izumi alternated as the primary burial sites for regional chiefs."Report on the Excavation of the ЕҢzono Site," Izumi City Education Board, 2014, pp. 1вҖ“4. In 2003, the mound was designated a historic site by Izumi City.{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/65/shiteibunkazai201804.pdf Izumi City Designated Cultural Properties]}} (Izumi City official website). File:Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun, zenkei.jpg|Full view of the mound

Entrance to the mound and informational sign | alternate_name = | group = | location = {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮ}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|еӨ§йҳӘеәң}}, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|30|30.40|N|135|26|32.25|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | type = {{ill|Hotategaigata Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} | size = Total mound length: 60m
Height: 7.7m | burial_chamber = Unknown | artifacts = Steatite trough, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments | period = Early [[5th century]] | designation = Izumi City Historic Site (2003) }} '''Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun''' (дҝЎеӨӘиІқеҗ№еұұеҸӨеўі) is a {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} located in {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮ}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|еӨ§йҳӘеәң}}, Japan. It has been designated a historic site by Izumi City. == Overview ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Lineage of Izumi Northern Chief Tombs"Lineage of Izumi Northern Chiefs," in {{ill|Izumi City History Volume 6|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮгҒ®жӯҙеҸІ6}}: Archaeology, Ancient, and Medieval, Izumi City Editorial Committee, Izumi City, Gyosei, 2013, pp. 65вҖ“70. ! colspan="2" | Group !! Tomb name !! Shape !! Size !! Period |- | North || || {{ill|Izumi Koganezuka Kofun|ja|е’Ңжіүй»„йҮ‘еЎҡеҸӨеўі}}|| {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|1=Zenpokoenfun}} || 94m || Late 4th century |- | || South || Marukasa-yama Kofun || Zenpokoenfun || 96m || Late 4th century |- | || South || Nabetsuka Kofun || {{ill|Empun|ja|еҶҶеўі|1=Round Kofun}} || 60вҖ“70m || Early 5th century |- | || South || ЕҢtsuka Kofun || Hotategaigata Kofun || 46m || Mid-5th century |- | North || || '''Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun''' || Hotategaigata Kofun || 60m || Early 5th century |- | North || || Kaniya-zuka Kofun || Hotategaigata Kofun || Unknown || Late 5th century |- | North || || {{ill|ЕҢzono Kofun|ja|еӨ§ең’еҸӨеўі}} || Hotategaigata Kofun || 50m || Late 5th century |- | North || || {{ill|Togidensha Kofun|ja|еҜҢжңЁи»ҠеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} || Zenpokoenfun || 48m || Mid-6th century |- | || South || Kitsunezuka Kofun || Zenpokoenfun || 58m || Late 6th century |}
Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun is located on a low terrace at the northwestern foot of the {{ill|Shinodayama Hills|ja|дҝЎеӨӘеұұдёҳйҷө}} in southern Osaka. The name "Kaibukiyama" derives from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|жұҹжҲёжҷӮд»Ј}} when farmers gathered here during the Chihara Revolt, using conch shells (hЕҚragai) as signals.Historical Site Information Board, Izumi City, 2005. Partial archaeological investigations have been conducted.ЕҢtani, Takeshi. "Kaibukiyama Kofun," in {{ill|Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|ja|ж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨеўіеӨ§иҫһе…ё}}, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. The mound is in the form of a short-tailed {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} with the short tail facing northwest. It measures approximately 60 meters in total length, with the rear circular section having a diameter of 50 meters and a height of 7.7 meters. The mound surface reveals {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|и‘әзҹі}} and {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments (both cylindrical and figurative). Surrounding the mound is a moat. Artifacts recovered include haniwa fragments and a steatite trough. The burial chamber's structure remains unknown. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the early [[5th century]], during the middle period of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. Archaeological studies suggest that the northern and southern burial clusters of Izumi alternated as the primary burial sites for regional chiefs."Report on the Excavation of the ЕҢzono Site," Izumi City Education Board, 2014, pp. 1вҖ“4. In 2003, the mound was designated a historic site by Izumi City.{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/65/shiteibunkazai201804.pdf Izumi City Designated Cultural Properties]}} (Izumi City official website). Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun is located on a low terrace at the northwestern foot of the {{ill|Shinodayama Hills|ja|дҝЎеӨӘеұұдёҳйҷө}} in southern Osaka. The name "Kaibukiyama" derives from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|жұҹжҲёжҷӮд»Ј}} when farmers gathered here during the Chihara Revolt, using conch shells (hЕҚragai) as signals.Historical Site Information Board, Izumi City, 2005. Partial archaeological investigations have been conducted.ЕҢtani, Takeshi. "Kaibukiyama Kofun," in {{ill|Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|ja|ж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨеўіеӨ§иҫһе…ё}}, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. The mound is in the form of a short-tailed {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} with the short tail facing northwest. It measures approximately 60 meters in total length, with the rear circular section having a diameter of 50 meters and a height of 7.7 meters. The mound surface reveals {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|и‘әзҹі}} and {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments (both cylindrical and figurative). Surrounding the mound is a moat. Artifacts recovered include haniwa fragments and a steatite trough. The burial chamber's structure remains unknown. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the early [[5th century]], during the middle period of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. Archaeological studies suggest that the northern and southern burial clusters of Izumi alternated as the primary burial sites for regional chiefs."Report on the Excavation of the ЕҢzono Site," Izumi City Education Board, 2014, pp. 1вҖ“4. In 2003, the mound was designated a historic site by Izumi City.{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/65/shiteibunkazai201804.pdf Izumi City Designated Cultural Properties]}} (Izumi City official website). Entrance to the mound and informational sign | alternate_name = | group = | location = {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮ}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|еӨ§йҳӘеәң}}, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|30|30.40|N|135|26|32.25|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | type = {{ill|Hotategaigata Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} | size = Total mound length: 60m
Height: 7.7m | burial_chamber = Unknown | artifacts = Steatite trough, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments | period = Early [[5th century]] | designation = Izumi City Historic Site (2003) }} '''Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun''' (дҝЎеӨӘиІқеҗ№еұұеҸӨеўі) is a {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} located in {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮ}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|еӨ§йҳӘеәң}}, Japan. It has been designated a historic site by Izumi City. == Overview ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Lineage of Izumi Northern Chief Tombs"Lineage of Izumi Northern Chiefs," in {{ill|Izumi City History Volume 6|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮгҒ®жӯҙеҸІ6}}: Archaeology, Ancient, and Medieval, Izumi City Editorial Committee, Izumi City, Gyosei, 2013, pp. 65вҖ“70. ! colspan="2" | Group !! Tomb name !! Shape !! Size !! Period |- | North || || {{ill|Izumi Koganezuka Kofun|ja|е’Ңжіүй»„йҮ‘еЎҡеҸӨеўі}}|| {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|1=Zenpokoenfun}} || 94m || Late 4th century |- | || South || Marukasa-yama Kofun || Zenpokoenfun || 96m || Late 4th century |- | || South || Nabetsuka Kofun || {{ill|Empun|ja|еҶҶеўі|1=Round Kofun}} || 60вҖ“70m || Early 5th century |- | || South || ЕҢtsuka Kofun || Hotategaigata Kofun || 46m || Mid-5th century |- | North || || '''Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun''' || Hotategaigata Kofun || 60m || Early 5th century |- | North || || Kaniya-zuka Kofun || Hotategaigata Kofun || Unknown || Late 5th century |- | North || || {{ill|ЕҢzono Kofun|ja|еӨ§ең’еҸӨеўі}} || Hotategaigata Kofun || 50m || Late 5th century |- | North || || {{ill|Togidensha Kofun|ja|еҜҢжңЁи»ҠеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} || Zenpokoenfun || 48m || Mid-6th century |- | || South || Kitsunezuka Kofun || Zenpokoenfun || 58m || Late 6th century |}
Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun is located on a low terrace at the northwestern foot of the {{ill|Shinodayama Hills|ja|дҝЎеӨӘеұұдёҳйҷө}} in southern Osaka. The name "Kaibukiyama" derives from the {{ill|Edo period|ja|жұҹжҲёжҷӮд»Ј}} when farmers gathered here during the Chihara Revolt, using conch shells (hЕҚragai) as signals.Historical Site Information Board, Izumi City, 2005. Partial archaeological investigations have been conducted.ЕҢtani, Takeshi. "Kaibukiyama Kofun," in {{ill|Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|ja|ж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨеўіеӨ§иҫһе…ё}}, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. The mound is in the form of a short-tailed {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} with the short tail facing northwest. It measures approximately 60 meters in total length, with the rear circular section having a diameter of 50 meters and a height of 7.7 meters. The mound surface reveals {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|и‘әзҹі}} and {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments (both cylindrical and figurative). Surrounding the mound is a moat. Artifacts recovered include haniwa fragments and a steatite trough. The burial chamber's structure remains unknown. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the early [[5th century]], during the middle period of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. Archaeological studies suggest that the northern and southern burial clusters of Izumi alternated as the primary burial sites for regional chiefs."Report on the Excavation of the ЕҢzono Site," Izumi City Education Board, 2014, pp. 1вҖ“4. In 2003, the mound was designated a historic site by Izumi City.{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/65/shiteibunkazai201804.pdf Izumi City Designated Cultural Properties]}} (Izumi City official website). Height: 7.7m | burial_chamber = Unknown | artifacts = Steatite trough, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} fragments | period = Early [[5th century]] | designation = Izumi City Historic Site (2003) }} '''Shinoda Kaibukiyama Kofun''' (дҝЎеӨӘиІқеҗ№еұұеҸӨеўі) is a {{ill|Scallop-shaped Kofun|ja|еёҶз«ӢиІқеҪўеҸӨеўі|1=Scallop Kofun}} located in {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|ja|е’ҢжіүеёӮ}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|еӨ§йҳӘеәң}}, Japan. It has been designated a historic site by Izumi City. == Overview ==
